questions.question: What is the fundamental distinction between duress at common law and undue influence in equity?

options.A: Duress applies only to contracts; undue influence applies only to gifts
options.B: Duress requires threats of physical violence or unlawful conduct; undue influence captures more subtle forms of pressure through exploitation of relationships of trust and confidence
options.C: Duress is actionable in damages; undue influence results only in rescission of the contract
options.D: There is no substantive distinction — both doctrines operate in the same way and are interchangeable

questions.explanation: The key distinction is in the nature of the pressure applied. Duress at common law requires threats of physical violence or threats of unlawful conduct that overbear the claimant's will. Undue influence in equity is broader — it captures more subtle forms of pressure arising from the exploitation of a relationship of trust and confidence. Equity intervenes where the influence, while not amounting to duress, was sufficient to deprive the party of their free will in entering the transaction.

questions.question: How many categories of undue influence are there, and what are they?

options.A: One category — actual undue influence, which requires proof of improper pressure in every case
options.B: Two categories — actual undue influence (Class 1) and presumed undue influence (Class 2), the latter being divided into Class 2A (automatic presumption) and Class 2B (factual presumption)
options.C: Three categories — express undue influence, implied undue influence, and constructive undue influence
options.D: Four categories — physical, psychological, financial, and relational undue influence

questions.explanation: There are two principal categories of undue influence. Class 1 (actual undue influence) requires the claimant to prove that the other party actually exerted undue influence — there is no presumption and the claimant bears the burden throughout. Class 2 (presumed undue influence) is divided into Class 2A (relationships where undue influence is automatically presumed by law, such as solicitor-client) and Class 2B (relationships where the presumption arises on the facts once a relationship of trust and confidence is established). This framework was authoritatively set out in Royal Bank of Scotland v Etridge (No. 2) [2001].

questions.question: Which of the following relationships falls within Class 2A — where undue influence is automatically presumed as a matter of law?

options.A: Employer and employee
options.B: Landlord and tenant
options.C: Solicitor and client
options.D: Business partners

questions.explanation: Class 2A covers relationships where undue influence is automatically presumed by law. The established categories include: solicitor and client, doctor and patient, parent and child (where the child is not emancipated), religious advisor and follower, and trustee and beneficiary. In these relationships, the law presumes — without the need for factual evidence — that one party is in a position to exert undue influence over the other. Employer-employee, landlord-tenant, and business partner relationships do not fall within this automatic category.

questions.question: What must a claimant establish to raise the presumption of undue influence under Class 2B?

options.A: That the defendant committed fraud in procuring the transaction
options.B: That there was a relationship of trust and confidence between the parties, and that the transaction calls for explanation because it is not readily explicable by the relationship
options.C: That the defendant was in a superior financial position and used that position to their advantage
options.D: That the claimant did not receive independent legal advice before entering the transaction

questions.explanation: For Class 2B presumed undue influence, the claimant must establish: (1) that they placed trust and confidence in the other party, and that party had a corresponding ascendancy or influence; and (2) that the transaction is not readily explicable by the nature of the relationship — that is, it 'calls for explanation'. Once these two elements are established, undue influence is presumed and the burden shifts to the defendant to rebut it by demonstrating that the transaction was the product of the claimant's free, informed, and independent will.

questions.question: Royal Bank of Scotland v Etridge (No. 2) [2001] is the leading House of Lords authority on undue influence. What is the central holding of Etridge in the banking context?

options.A: That banks are never liable for the consequences of undue influence between spouses
options.B: That a bank is put on inquiry whenever a wife offers to stand surety for the debts of her husband or his business, and must take reasonable steps to ensure she has received independent legal advice
options.C: That undue influence is presumed in all surety transactions regardless of the relationship between the parties
options.D: That independent legal advice is a complete defence for any bank involved in a transaction tainted by undue influence

questions.explanation: In Royal Bank of Scotland v Etridge (No. 2) [2001], Lord Nicholls held that a bank is 'put on inquiry' whenever a wife (or more broadly, any person in a non-commercial relationship) stands surety for another's debts. When put on inquiry, the bank must take reasonable steps to ensure that the surety has received independent legal advice and understands the nature and effect of the transaction. The bank should require the solicitor acting for the surety to confirm that advice has been given. This decision significantly reformed the law in this area.

questions.question: When is a bank said to be 'put on inquiry' under the Etridge framework?

options.A: Whenever a customer provides security for another party's debt, regardless of the nature of the relationship
options.B: Only when the bank has actual knowledge of undue influence between the parties
options.C: When the transaction is not on its face to the financial advantage of the surety and there is a non-commercial relationship between the surety and the principal debtor
options.D: When the surety is a limited company or partnership

questions.explanation: Under the Etridge framework, a bank is put on inquiry when two conditions are present: (1) the transaction is not on its face to the financial advantage of the surety — that is, the surety gains nothing from the transaction; and (2) there is a non-commercial relationship between the surety and the principal debtor (such as husband and wife, or cohabiting partners). The bank need not have actual knowledge of undue influence — the combination of a non-commercial relationship and an apparent lack of personal financial benefit to the surety is sufficient to put the bank on inquiry.

questions.question: In Ulster Bank v Fitzgerald [2001], Laffoy J considered the application of the Etridge principles in Ireland. What was the initial Irish position established in that case?

options.A: That Irish law fully adopted all aspects of Etridge and imposed strict obligations on banks
options.B: That Irish law does not impose on banks any obligation to ensure independent advice is obtained in surety transactions
options.C: That the Etridge framework was unconstitutional as a disproportionate interference with commercial freedom
options.D: That banks in Ireland are always vicariously liable for any undue influence exercised by their clients

questions.explanation: In Ulster Bank v Fitzgerald [2001], Laffoy J held that, as a matter of Irish law, banks do not have any obligation to ensure that a surety receives independent legal advice before standing surety for another's debts. This represented a narrower approach than Etridge. However, this position was subsequently revisited in Ulster Bank v Roche [2012] where Clarke J significantly revised this initial position to bring Irish law more closely into alignment with the underlying principles of Etridge.

questions.question: How did Ulster Bank v Roche [2012] modify the position established in Ulster Bank v Fitzgerald [2001]?

options.A: It confirmed that Irish law had fully rejected the Etridge framework
options.B: Clarke J held that the general principle underlying Etridge — that banks must inquire where aware of a non-commercial element to a guarantee — does represent Irish law, even if not all precise parameters of Etridge apply
options.C: It held that the Etridge framework only applies to mortgage transactions and not to guarantee or surety arrangements
options.D: It abolished all judicial scrutiny of bank behaviour in surety transactions, leaving the matter to be regulated by the Central Bank

questions.explanation: In Ulster Bank v Roche [2012], Clarke J significantly revised the earlier position from Fitzgerald. He held that while not all the precise parameters of Etridge necessarily apply in Ireland, the fundamental principle that a bank is put on inquiry — and must take reasonable steps — when it is aware of facts suggesting a non-commercial element to a guarantee, does represent Irish law. This brought the Irish position meaningfully closer to the Etridge framework, confirming that banks cannot ignore obvious signs of potential undue influence.

questions.question: In ACC Bank v Connolly, the Irish courts confirmed an important limiting principle regarding the role of independent legal advice. What was that principle?

options.A: That independent legal advice is always required before a bank can enforce any security
options.B: That the question of independent legal advice only arises if undue influence is first established — if there is no undue influence, the absence of independent advice is irrelevant
options.C: That independent legal advice provided by the bank's own solicitor satisfies the Etridge requirements
options.D: That independent legal advice must be provided in writing and witnessed by a commissioner for oaths

questions.explanation: ACC Bank v Connolly confirmed an important sequencing principle: the obligation on a bank to ensure independent legal advice was obtained is not a free-standing requirement. It is only relevant if undue influence is first established on the facts. If the claimant cannot demonstrate that undue influence was actually exercised (Class 1) or presumed (Class 2), then the absence of independent legal advice does not itself vitiate the transaction. Independent legal advice is relevant to the question of whether the bank took adequate steps once put on inquiry — not as a standalone condition.

questions.question: A wife agrees to mortgage the family home to secure her husband's business debts. She later claims she did not understand what she was signing and was pressured by her husband. The bank had notice that the transaction was not for her benefit. What must the bank show to enforce the mortgage?

options.A: That the wife signed the mortgage voluntarily and received consideration
options.B: That the bank took reasonable steps to ensure the wife received independent legal advice and understood the nature and effect of the transaction, in accordance with the Etridge and Roche principles
options.C: That the husband confirmed the wife was acting freely
options.D: That the mortgage was witnessed by a solicitor in the wife's presence

questions.explanation: On these facts, the bank is put on inquiry under the Etridge/Roche framework: the transaction is not to the wife's financial benefit, and the bank is aware of the non-commercial nature of the relationship. To enforce the mortgage, the bank must demonstrate that it took reasonable steps to satisfy itself that the wife received independent legal advice — from a solicitor acting for her alone, not for the bank or the husband — and that she understood the nature and effect of the transaction before signing. Failure to take such steps may result in the mortgage being set aside.

questions.question: A patient, in gratitude to her surgeon after a successful operation, transfers substantial savings to the surgeon as a gift. The patient later seeks to set aside the transfer. Which category of undue influence is most relevant?

options.A: Class 1 — actual undue influence, because the surgeon must have actively pressured the patient
options.B: Class 2A — presumed undue influence, because the doctor-patient relationship is one where undue influence is automatically presumed by law
options.C: Class 2B — presumed undue influence based on the factual relationship of trust and confidence
options.D: No undue influence, because gratitude to a surgeon is a commercially explicable reason for making a gift

questions.explanation: The doctor-patient relationship falls within Class 2A — relationships where undue influence is automatically presumed as a matter of law. The law recognises that the trust and vulnerability inherent in this relationship creates a potential for exploitation. Consequently, without needing to prove any specific act of pressure, the patient can invoke the presumption of undue influence. The burden then shifts to the surgeon to rebut the presumption by showing, for example, that the patient received independent advice and acted entirely of their own free will.

questions.question: Once the presumption of undue influence under Class 2 is raised, how can the defendant rebut it?

options.A: By proving that the claimant was of above-average intelligence and therefore could not have been influenced
options.B: By demonstrating that the transaction was the result of the claimant's free, fully informed, and independent judgment — typically evidenced by independent legal advice
options.C: By obtaining a sworn affidavit from the claimant confirming that the transaction was made voluntarily
options.D: The presumption under Class 2 is irrebuttable and cannot be displaced by any evidence

questions.explanation: Once the presumption of undue influence under Class 2 is raised, the burden shifts to the defendant to rebut it. The defendant must show that the transaction was the product of the claimant's free, fully informed, and independent judgment. The most effective way to do this is to demonstrate that the claimant received competent, independent legal advice that explained the nature and effect of the transaction before it was entered into. This rebuts the presumption by showing that any influence was not decisive in procuring the transaction.

questions.question: What is the consequence if undue influence is successfully established in respect of a transaction?

options.A: The transaction is void ab initio and treated as if it never existed
options.B: The transaction is voidable — it may be set aside (rescinded) by the court, subject to the usual bars to rescission
options.C: The defendant is automatically liable in damages for any loss suffered by the claimant
options.D: The transaction is enforceable but the court will substitute fair and reasonable terms in place of the agreed terms

questions.explanation: Where undue influence is established, the transaction is voidable — not void. This means it subsists until set aside by the court through rescission. The claimant must seek rescission, and the court may refuse it if one of the bars to rescission applies — for example, if the parties cannot be restored to their pre-contractual positions (restitutio in integrum is impossible), if third parties have acquired rights for value, if the claimant has affirmed the transaction, or if there has been undue delay (laches).

questions.question: Which of the following transactions would NOT automatically raise a presumption of undue influence under Class 2A?

options.A: A gift by a client to their solicitor
options.B: A transfer of property by a patient to their doctor
options.C: A loan by an adult child to their parent
options.D: A gift by a follower to their religious advisor

questions.explanation: The automatic Class 2A presumption arises in specified relationships: solicitor-client, doctor-patient, parent-child (where the presumption operates in favour of the child against the parent, not the reverse), religious advisor-follower, and trustee-beneficiary. A loan by an adult child to a parent does not fall within any of these established categories. To raise undue influence in this case, the adult child would need to rely on Class 2B and prove a relationship of trust and confidence on the facts. Options A, B, and D all fall within established Class 2A categories.

questions.question: What does it mean for a transaction to 'call for explanation' in the context of Class 2B undue influence?

options.A: The claimant must provide a detailed written explanation of why they entered the transaction
options.B: The transaction must be explicable on the basis of the ordinary incidents of the relationship without involving undue influence
options.C: The transaction is not readily explicable by the nature of the relationship — it is so disadvantageous that an ordinary person in that position would not have entered into it without some form of improper pressure
options.D: The defendant must explain the transaction to the court in a witness statement before the presumption can arise

questions.explanation: For the Class 2B presumption to arise, the transaction must 'call for explanation' — meaning it is not readily explicable by the nature of the relationship between the parties. If the transaction is so one-sided or disadvantageous that an ordinary person in that relationship would not have entered into it without some form of improper influence, it calls for explanation. Once this threshold is met, and the relationship of trust and confidence is established, the presumption arises and the burden shifts to the defendant to provide an innocent explanation.

questions.question: A solicitor advises a client on the sale of their farm. The client, an elderly person in poor health, transfers the farm to the solicitor for a consideration significantly below market value. The solicitor claims the client insisted on the arrangement. What is the most likely legal analysis?

options.A: The transaction is valid because the client was legally capable and the solicitor has no duty of care in personal dealings
options.B: The solicitor-client relationship falls within Class 2A; undue influence is automatically presumed and the solicitor must rebut it by showing the client received independent advice from a separate solicitor
options.C: Class 2B applies and the client must first prove a relationship of trust and confidence before any presumption arises
options.D: The transaction cannot be challenged because it is evidenced in writing and signed by both parties

questions.explanation: The solicitor-client relationship is a Class 2A relationship, meaning undue influence is automatically presumed. The transaction — a below-market-value transfer from client to solicitor — is also highly suspect. The solicitor must rebut the presumption by demonstrating that the client received genuinely independent legal advice from a separate solicitor (not involved in the transaction) and that the transfer was the product of the client's own free and fully informed decision. The fact that the client 'insisted' is unlikely, without more, to rebut the presumption.

questions.question: For FE-1 purposes, which of the following is the most common factual scenario in undue influence exam questions?

options.A: A company director guaranteeing a company's commercial bank loan
options.B: A family member — typically a spouse or parent — standing surety for another's business debts, with a bank seeking to enforce the security
options.C: A buyer and seller in a commercial property transaction where terms are disputed
options.D: A trustee seeking to benefit from trust property against the beneficiaries' wishes

questions.explanation: The most common FE-1 undue influence scenario involves a family member — typically a wife, husband, or parent — providing a guarantee or mortgage over family assets to secure another family member's business debts. The key issues are: identifying the category of undue influence, assessing whether the bank was put on inquiry under the Etridge/Roche principles, and determining whether adequate steps were taken regarding independent legal advice. Candidates should structure their answer around these three stages.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about Class 1 actual undue influence is correct?

options.A: Class 1 requires the claimant to be in a presumed position of vulnerability by law
options.B: Under Class 1, the claimant must affirmatively prove that the other party actually exerted improper influence — there is no presumption and the burden does not shift
options.C: Class 1 and Class 2A differ only in that Class 1 requires proof that the transaction called for explanation
options.D: Class 1 only applies to transactions involving physical pressure or threats

questions.explanation: Under Class 1 actual undue influence, there is no presumption of any kind. The claimant bears the burden of proving throughout that the other party actually exerted improper influence over them — for example, through persistent pressure, manipulation, or exploitation of emotional dependence — and that this influence was operative in procuring the transaction. Unlike Class 2, the claimant cannot rely on the nature of the relationship alone to shift the burden to the defendant.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a bar to rescission in an undue influence claim?

options.A: Restitutio in integrum is impossible — the parties cannot be restored to their pre-transaction positions
options.B: Affirmation — the claimant has, with full knowledge of the undue influence, treated the transaction as binding
options.C: Third party rights — a bona fide purchaser for value without notice has acquired rights under the transaction
options.D: The claimant failed to take independent legal advice before entering the transaction

questions.explanation: The failure to take independent legal advice is not itself a bar to rescission. The bars to rescission in an undue influence claim are: (1) impossibility of restitutio in integrum — the parties cannot be restored to their pre-transaction positions; (2) affirmation — the claimant has confirmed the transaction with full knowledge of the undue influence; (3) laches — unreasonable delay in seeking rescission; and (4) third party rights — a bona fide purchaser for value without notice has acquired interests under the transaction. The absence of independent legal advice goes to establishing the claim, not to barring the remedy.

questions.question: A husband and wife run a business together. The wife agrees to mortgage the jointly owned family home to secure a bank overdraft for the business. Both are directors. The bank did not advise the wife to take independent legal advice. Is the bank put on inquiry?

options.A: Yes — because there is a husband-wife relationship, the bank is always put on inquiry regardless of the wife's involvement in the business
options.B: No — because both spouses are directors of the business, the transaction has a commercial rationale on its face and the bank is not put on inquiry
options.C: Yes — because all mortgage transactions involving a family home automatically trigger the inquiry obligation
options.D: No — because Etridge only applies to English banks and has no application in Ireland

questions.explanation: Where both spouses are directors and active participants in the business, the transaction has a commercial rationale on its face — the wife stands to benefit from the business overdraft as a co-director. In these circumstances, the bank is not put on inquiry under the Etridge/Roche framework because the transaction is commercially explicable on the wife's side. The inquiry obligation arises where the transaction is not to the apparent financial advantage of the surety and the relationship is non-commercial. Joint directors involved in the same business do not fall within this category.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about undue influence in Irish law is CORRECT?

options.A: Irish courts have completely rejected the Etridge framework and apply a purely domestic test for undue influence
options.B: The fundamental principle of Etridge — that a bank must inquire when aware of a non-commercial element to a guarantee — was accepted as part of Irish law by Clarke J in Ulster Bank v Roche [2012]
options.C: Irish law presumes undue influence in all transactions between spouses, without any need to identify a specific category
options.D: Under Irish law, a bank is never liable for failing to ensure a surety received independent legal advice because this is the responsibility of the surety's own solicitor

questions.explanation: The correct statement is that the fundamental principle of Etridge was accepted as part of Irish law by Clarke J in Ulster Bank v Roche [2012]. While the earlier decision of Ulster Bank v Fitzgerald [2001] initially suggested a narrower approach, Clarke J in Roche confirmed that the general principle — that a bank must take reasonable steps when put on inquiry by a non-commercial guarantee situation — does apply in Ireland. Not all precise parameters of Etridge necessarily apply, but the core principle does. Irish courts apply their own analysis, drawing heavily on Etridge as persuasive authority.
